target.name: indole-derived combretastatin molecule
target.motivation: The application of the developed method using a single ligand scaffold is demonstrated in the short synthesis of an indole-derived combretastatin molecule. This important molecule (23) can be synthesized following three consecutive C–H borylations as the key synthetic steps in good yield using our developed methods.

success_criteria.free_text: Short synthesis using the developed single ligand (7-Aza-BPY) and good yield.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis of the indole-derived combretastatin molecule (23) is achieved through three consecutive C-H borylations followed by arylation and deprotection steps. The strategy leverages the regioselective C-H borylation capabilities of the developed single ligand (L1: 7-Aza-BPY) to functionalize the indole core at C3, C5, and C2 positions sequentially.

thought.background: Transition metal-catalyzed C-H bond functionalization is a key synthetic tool, but direct functionalization of different types of C–H bonds by a single catalyst remains a long-standing challenge. Previous methods often require additional steps for substrate modification or different ligand/catalyst systems for ortho, meta, and para selectivity. Iridium-catalyzed C-H borylation is known to be influenced by ligand design.
thought.gap: No general method is available to functionalize all three distinct C–H bonds (para–meta–ortho) of a given molecular scaffold using a single ligand system.
thought.decision: To address the challenge of functionalizing all three distinct C–H bonds (para, meta & ortho) within diverse classes of arenes, we will introduce a single ligand strategy (7-Aza-BPY, L1) where site-selectivity can be modulated through rational variation of additives under iridium catalysis. We anticipate that the azaindole scaffold, being electronically rich, could facilitate site-selectivity through noncovalent interactions.

outcome.observation: Hypothesis formed to develop a single ligand (L1: 7-Aza-BPY) for regiodivergent C-H borylation.

evidence.text_span: In this work, we address the aforementioned challenges by introducing a single ligand strategy, wherein site-selectivity can be precisely modulated through the rational variation of additives. This approach establishes a versatile platform that enables controlled activation of electronically distinct C–H bonds within a single catalytic framework (Fig. 1C). In the field of iridium-catalyzed C-H borylation8,51, ligands have been pivotal in achieving excellent reactivity, and siteselectivity. Innovative ligand designs are crucial for advancing borylation chemistry, requiring a diimine-type backbone (e.g., bipyridine) for Ir binding, and a noncovalent procreator for guiding selectivity. Drawing inspiration from diverse weak interactions52–55, we have developed 7-Aza-BPY (L1) for the iridium-catalyzed C-H borylation. We anticipated that azaindole being an electronically rich scaffold and bioisostere of indole ring56, could independently or synergistically with other additives facilitate the desired site-selectivity through noncovalent interactions within the realm of C-H borylation (Fig. 1D).

thought.background: Initial studies with L1 and [Ir(cod)OMe]2 showed non-selective borylations. The goal is to achieve regioselectivity.
thought.gap: Identify additives that can induce regioselectivity (para, meta, or ortho) in the C-H borylation of arenes using ligand L1.
thought.decision: To identify suitable additives, various alkali metal salts will be screened using aromatic amide (1a) as a model substrate, in quantities equivalent to the ligand loading (3.0 mol%).

outcome.observation: The incorporation of KOtBu (3.0 mol%) resulted in pronounced para-selective borylation (82% para selectivity) with almost quantitative conversion.

evidence.text_span: Initially, our comprehensive study using the developed ligand (L1: 7- Aza-BPY) in presence of $[ \mathbf { I r ( c o d ) ( O M e ) } ] _ { 2 }$ with diverse classes of arenes revealed predominantly non-selective borylations (See Fig. S1 in SI for details). Subsequently, using aromatic amide (1a) as the model substrate, we introduced various additives. Consequently, we incorporated several alkali metal salts in quantities equivalent to the ligand loading $( 3 . 0 \mathrm { m o l \% } )$ . This revealed very surprising and intriguing results. The incorporation of various alkali metal salts, including KOt Bu, NaOt Bu, KOPiv, KOAc, NaOAc and KF was observed to exert a notable impact on the borylation reaction (entries 3-8, see Table S1 in SI for details) (Fig. 2A). Notably, the pronounced para-selective $82 \%$ para selectivity) borylation was achieved using KOt Bu (3.0 mol%) with almost quantitative conversion (Fig. 2A, entry 3).

thought.gap: Investigate if varying KOtBu loading can switch selectivity between para and meta C-H bonds.
thought.decision: To explore the effect of KOtBu concentration, an extensive titration with KOtBu up to 19.0 mol% loading will be performed under the same reaction conditions.

outcome.observation: With 2.0 mol% KOtBu, the best para selectivity (para/meta/ortho = 97/3/0) was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Then, we were curious about the outcomes if we vary the loading of KOt Bu under the same reaction conditions. Accordingly, an extensive KOt Bu titration was carried out up to $1 9 . 0 \ : \mathrm { m o l \% }$ loading (Fig. 2B), which disclosed an unusual switching of selectivity between the para and meta C-H bonds of the substrate (1a). We observed from this titration experiment that while the best para selectivity (para/meta/ortho $\mathbf { \Sigma } = 9 7 / 3 / 0 )$ is obtained with 2.0 mol% KOt Bu, the best meta selectivity (para/meta/ortho $= 4 /$ 96/0) is achieved with $1 4 . 0 \ : \mathrm { m o l \% }$ KOt Bu (Fig. 2B).

thought.background: KOtBu titration showed switchable selectivity. 2.0 mol% KOtBu gave para-selectivity.
thought.gap: Identify the optimal KOtBu loading for meta-selectivity.
thought.decision: Continue the KOtBu titration to find the optimal loading for meta-selective borylation.

outcome.observation: With 14.0 mol% KOtBu, the best meta selectivity (para/meta/ortho = 4/96/0) was achieved.

evidence.text_span: We observed from this titration experiment that while the best para selectivity (para/meta/ortho $\mathbf { \Sigma } = 9 7 / 3 / 0 )$ is obtained with 2.0 mol% KOt Bu, the best meta selectivity (para/meta/ortho $= 4 /$ 96/0) is achieved with $1 4 . 0 \ : \mathrm { m o l \% }$ KOt Bu (Fig. 2B).

thought.background: Varying KOtBu loading leads to switchable para and meta selectivity.
thought.gap: Understand the mechanism behind the switchable selectivity.
thought.decision: Hypothesize that varying KOtBu loading might form different in situ complexes, contributing to the switchable selectivities. Inspired by Marder's work, envision that B2pin2, KOtBu, L1, and iridium metal might form diverse catalytic systems.

outcome.observation: Hypothesis formed that different in situ complexes are responsible for switchable selectivity.

evidence.text_span: After achieving notable switchable selectivity, we hypothesized that varying the KOt Bu loading might form different in situ complexes, contributing to the switchable selectivities. Subsequently, inspired by Marder’s pioneering work62, we envisioned that $\mathsf { B } _ { 2 } \mathsf { p i n } _ { 2 }$ , in conjunction with KOt Bu and ligand L1, alongside with iridium metal, might form diverse catalytic systems that are crucial for achieving flexible remote selectivity switches.

thought.background: Hypothesis that in situ complexes are responsible for selectivity. Need to investigate the nature of these complexes.
thought.gap: Characterize the complex formed from B2pin2 and KOtBu.
thought.decision: Prepare 'Complex-A' from B2pin2 and KOtBu and characterize it using 11B NMR.

outcome.observation: Complex-A was prepared and characterized. 11B NMR showed two distinct boron atoms (sp2 at 37.9 ppm and sp3 at 5.3 ppm), confirming its formation.

evidence.text_span: Following this hypothesis, we prepared Complex-A (Fig. 2C), which confirmed that the two boron atoms are completely different in nature (one boron atom is $s p ^ { 2 }$ , giving 37.9 ppm in ${ } ^ { 11 } \mathbf { B }$ NMR and the other one is $s p ^ { 3 }$ coordinated by tert-butoxide that resulted in 5.3 ppm in ${ } ^ { 11 } { \bf B }$ NMR) (Fig. 2C).

thought.background: Complex-A was identified. Need to confirm its role in para-selectivity.
thought.gap: Determine if Complex-A can act as an additive to achieve para-selective borylation.
thought.decision: Perform a control experiment using Complex-A as an additive instead of KOtBu for para-selective borylation.

outcome.observation: Using Complex-A (1.3 mol%) as an additive afforded 98% para selectivity, which was even better than the 2.0 mol% KOtBu result. This indicated that in situ generated Complex-A is responsible for para selectivity.

evidence.text_span: Next, to understand the role of Complex-A, we performed a control experiment using Complex-A as an additive instead of the KOt Bu, which afforded $98 \%$ para selectivity that is even better than the outcomes of the 2.0 mol% KOt Bu result (Fig. 2D). Thus, this control experiment indicated that the in situ generated Complex-A with lower KOt Bu loading is primely responsible for the para selectivity. Hence, the para borylation was performed using Complex-A $( 1 . 3 \mathrm { m o l \% } )$ as our optimized additive and the meta borylation was conducted using 14.0 mol% KOt Bu as the optimized additive.

thought.background: Para and meta selective borylation conditions were established using KOtBu and Complex-A. Now, need to find conditions for ortho-selective borylation.
thought.gap: Identify an additive that can induce ortho-selectivity.
thought.decision: Examine the borylation reaction with a spectrum of organic bases as additives, starting with DABCO (3.0 mol%).

outcome.observation: Replacing KOtBu with DABCO (3.0 mol%) changed the selectivity from remote to proximal ortho selectivity (97% ortho selectivity with 86% conversion).

evidence.text_span: After conducting the borylation reaction employing various alkali metal salts, we then examined the borylation reaction across a spectrum of organic bases (Fig. 2E, see Table S4 in SI for details). We found that while the inorganic base (KOt Bu) afforded remote para and meta selective borylation depending on the loadings of additive and developed ligand (L1), surprisingly, replacing the inorganic additive KOt Bu with organic base DABCO $( 3 . 0 \mathrm { m o l \% }$ , entry 1) changed the whole scenario from remote to proximal ortho selectivity $( 9 7 \%$ ortho selectivity with $86 \%$ conversion).

thought.background: DABCO showed ortho-selectivity at 3.0 mol%. Urotropine also showed promise.
thought.gap: Optimize the loading of DABCO and urotropine for enhanced ortho-selectivity and conversion.
thought.decision: Conduct experiments by varying the loading of DABCO and urotropine to find optimal conditions for ortho-selective borylation.

outcome.observation: With 5.0 mol% DABCO, even higher ortho selectivity (99%) and better conversion (92%) were achieved. Similarly, 5.0 mol% urotropine led to comparable selectivity with slightly reduced conversion.

evidence.text_span: Notably, the inclusion of $3 . 0 \mathrm { m o l \% }$ urotropine also yielded a remarkable $9 8 \%$ ortho-selectivity (entry 2). Seeking further enhancements, we conducted experiments by varying the loading of DABCO and urotropine. With $5 . 0 \mathrm { m o l \% }$ DABCO, we achieved even higher ortho selectivity $( 9 9 \% )$ and better conversion $( 9 2 \% )$ . Similarly, using $5 . 0 \mathrm { m o l \% }$ urotropine led to comparable selectivity, albeit with a slightly reduced conversion (See SI for other additive screening).

thought.background: Optimized conditions for para, meta, and ortho C-H borylation were established using a single ligand L1 and different additives (Complex-A for para, KOtBu for meta, DABCO/Urotropine for ortho).
thought.gap: Demonstrate the applicability of these conditions for the short synthesis of an indole-derived combretastatin molecule.
thought.decision: Apply the developed ortho-borylation conditions to N-amide indole (16) to achieve C2-selective borylation.

outcome.observation: N-amide indole (16) yielded 100% C2 selectivity under ortho borylation conditions, producing intermediate (18).

evidence.text_span: For further application of our developed method using a single ligand (7-Aza-BPY), we targeted N-amide indole (16, due to the prevalence of C3-functionalized indole-containing drugs) for the regioselective borylation (Fig. 6C). We observed that N-amide indole (16) under our developed para borylation conditions yielded $9 3 \%$ C5 selectivity and complete $( 1 0 0 \% )$ ) C2 selectivity under ortho borylation conditions (Fig. 6C). Notably, while C5-substituted indoles are common in natural products, borylation at the C5 position is underexplored due to multiple challenges63. Moreover, achieving C2 borylation at such a sterically hindered position presents challenges. But our developed single ligand strategy has successfully demonstrated highly regioselective switchable proximal and remote borylation of N-amide indole moiety.

thought.background: C2-borylated indole (18) was obtained. The target molecule (23) requires C3, C5, and C2 borylations followed by arylation and deprotection.
thought.gap: Synthesize the first intermediate (20) by C3 borylation of N-amide indole (19) followed by arylation.
thought.decision: Apply the developed borylation and arylation methods to N-amide indole (19) to obtain intermediate (20).

outcome.observation: The first intermediate (20) was achieved from N-amide indole (19) by C3 borylation followed by arylation in good yield.

evidence.text_span: For example, the first intermediate (20) can be achieved from N-amide indole (19, $\mathsf { R } = \mathsf { H } ,$ ) by the C3 borylation followed by the arylation. The second intermediate (21) can be prepared by the C5 selective borylation followed by a routine methoxylation in good yield. Subsequently, the third intermediate (22) can be prepared by the C2 selective borylation followed by the heteroarylation. The target molecule (23) can be achieved in $9 5 \%$ yield by a simple deprotection from the intermediate (22).

thought.background: Intermediate (20) was synthesized. Next step is to prepare intermediate (21).
thought.gap: Synthesize the second intermediate (21) by C5 selective borylation followed by methoxylation.
thought.decision: Apply the developed C5 selective borylation and a routine methoxylation to obtain intermediate (21).

outcome.observation: The second intermediate (21) was prepared by C5 selective borylation followed by a routine methoxylation in good yield.

evidence.text_span: For example, the first intermediate (20) can be achieved from N-amide indole (19, $\mathsf { R } = \mathsf { H } ,$ ) by the C3 borylation followed by the arylation. The second intermediate (21) can be prepared by the C5 selective borylation followed by a routine methoxylation in good yield. Subsequently, the third intermediate (22) can be prepared by the C2 selective borylation followed by the heteroarylation. The target molecule (23) can be achieved in $9 5 \%$ yield by a simple deprotection from the intermediate (22).

thought.background: Intermediate (21) was synthesized. Next step is to prepare intermediate (22).
thought.gap: Synthesize the third intermediate (22) by C2 selective borylation followed by heteroarylation.
thought.decision: Apply the developed C2 selective borylation and heteroarylation to obtain intermediate (22).

outcome.observation: The third intermediate (22) was prepared by C2 selective borylation followed by heteroarylation.

evidence.text_span: For example, the first intermediate (20) can be achieved from N-amide indole (19, $\mathsf { R } = \mathsf { H } ,$ ) by the C3 borylation followed by the arylation. The second intermediate (21) can be prepared by the C5 selective borylation followed by a routine methoxylation in good yield. Subsequently, the third intermediate (22) can be prepared by the C2 selective borylation followed by the heteroarylation. The target molecule (23) can be achieved in $9 5 \%$ yield by a simple deprotection from the intermediate (22).

thought.background: Intermediate (22) was synthesized, which is a precursor to the target molecule (23).
thought.gap: Convert intermediate (22) to the final indole-derived combretastatin molecule (23) through deprotection.
thought.decision: Perform a simple deprotection step on intermediate (22) to obtain the target molecule (23).

outcome.observation: The target molecule (23) was achieved in 95% yield by a simple deprotection from intermediate (22).

evidence.text_span: For example, the first intermediate (20) can be achieved from N-amide indole (19, $\mathsf { R } = \mathsf { H } ,$ ) by the C3 borylation followed by the arylation. The second intermediate (21) can be prepared by the C5 selective borylation followed by a routine methoxylation in good yield. Subsequently, the third intermediate (22) can be prepared by the C2 selective borylation followed by the heteroarylation. The target molecule (23) can be achieved in $9 5 \%$ yield by a simple deprotection from the intermediate (22).

validation.significance: The developed method allows for the regiodivergent functionalization of all three C–H bonds (para, meta, and ortho) of arenes using a single ligand framework (7-Aza-BPY) by varying additives. This approach is versatile, applicable to diverse substrate classes, and enables the selective installation of functional groups. Its utility is demonstrated in the late-stage C–H borylation of biologically important scaffolds and in the short synthesis of an indole-derived combretastatin molecule, highlighting its potential for medicinal chemistry, total synthesis, and industrial applications.

source.title: Iridium catalyzed regiodivergent functionalization of all three C-H bonds of arenes by a single ligand.
source.authors: Das J, Hoque ME, Hassan MMM, Guria S, Dey S, Ma J, Deng Z, Liang Y, Chattopadhyay B.
